perturbation.original_text: ESSI's products do, and will, conform at all times to all applicable federal, state and local laws, rules, regulations, ordinances, and other enactments and industry standards, including, but not limited to, those relating to product safety.
perturbation.changed_text: ESSI's products will endeavor to conform to applicable federal, state and local laws, rules, regulations, ordinances, and other enactments and industry standards, including those relating to product safety, to the extent reasonably practicable.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear warranty of compliance with all applicable laws. The modified text weakens this warranty by introducing the phrases 'endeavor to conform' and 'to the extent reasonably practicable,' creating ambiguity about the level of required compliance. This could be interpreted as allowing for less than full compliance if it is deemed impractical, undermining the original assurance of full legal compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: MCL § 445.903(1)(c) prohibits representing that goods or services have characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities that they do not have, or that a person has a sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that the person does not have. By weakening the assurance of compliance with laws related to product safety and other regulations, the modified text increases the risk that ESSI's products may not actually conform to the standards they are represented to meet, potentially misleading consumers and violating this section of the MCPA. The original clause is a clear representation that ESSI will conform, whereas the modified clause opens ESSI up to the interpretation that it 'attempts' to comply with applicable laws, thereby creating a loophole where they can make claims that the product meets certain standards without fully complying with them.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.903 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.903 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 331 of 1976 Previous Section Next Section MICHIGAN CONSUMER PROTECTION ACT (EXCERPT) Act 331 of 1976 445.903 Unfair, unconscionable, or deceptive methods, acts, or practices in conduct of trade or commerce; rules; applicability of subsection (1)(hh). Sec. 3. (1) Unfair, unconscionable, or deceptive methods, acts, or practices in the conduct of trade or commerce are unlawful and are defined as follows: (a) Causing a probability of confusion or misunderstanding as to the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services. (b) Using deceptive representations or deceptive designations of geographic origin in connection with goods or services. (c) Representing that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities that they do not have or that a person has sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that he or she does not have. (d) Representing that goods are new if they are deteriorated, altered, reconditioned, used, or secondhand. (e) Representing that goods or services are of a particular standard, quality, or grade, or that goods are of a particular style or model, if they are of another. (f) Disparaging the goods, services, business, or reputation of another by false or misleading representation of fact. (g) Advertising or representing goods or services with intent not to dispose of those goods or services as advertised or represented. (h) Advertising goods or services with intent not to supply reasonably expectable public demand, unless the advertisement discloses a limitation of quantity in immediate conjunction with the advertised goods or services. (i) Making false or misleading statements of fact concerning the reasons for, existence of, or amounts of price reductions. (j) Representing that a part, replacement, or repair service is needed when it is not. (k) Representing to a party to whom goods
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding ESSI's compliance with applicable laws by using the phrases 'endeavor to conform' and 'to the extent reasonably practicable.' This weakens the original assurance of full legal compliance. MCL § 445.903(1)(c), as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, prohibits 'Representing that goods or services have...characteristics...that they do not have.' By suggesting ESSI will 'endeavor' to comply, there is a risk their products may not fully conform to the standards they are represented to meet. This creates a loophole where ESSI can make claims without fully complying, contradicting the law. The original text represents a clear promise of compliance, which is significantly undermined by the modification.

perturbation.original_text: Talent may not, at any time, individually, or through his agent, manager, family, friends, or associates publish a press release relative to ESSI without first obtaining the written consent and approval of ESSI's management and counsel. In the event Talent does publish any such press release, this Agreement shall be null and void and any outstanding balance owed to Talent shall be voided and the 1,000,000 Shares of ESSI common stock issued to Talent returned to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Talent may not, without making a good faith effort to obtain written consent and approval of ESSI's management and counsel, publish a press release relating to ESSI. In the event Talent does publish any such press release, this Agreement may be reviewed by ESSI for potential modification.
perturbation.explanation: The original text is a strict prohibition on Talent publishing press releases without ESSI's approval, with severe consequences for violation. The modified text introduces ambiguity in two ways: 1) It replaces the absolute prohibition with a 'good faith effort' requirement, which is subjective and difficult to define. What constitutes a 'good faith effort'? 2) It replaces the automatic termination clause with a provision for 'potential modification,' which is vague and leaves the consequences of unauthorized press releases uncertain. This change introduces wiggle room where previously none existed.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Michigan Contract Law - Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While not codified in statute, Michigan, like many jurisdictions, recognizes an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in contracts. The original clause, while restrictive, is clear and unambiguous. By softening the requirement to 'good faith effort' and replacing automatic termination with 'potential modification', the modified clause introduces the *potential* for ESSI to act unfairly. For example, ESSI could arbitrarily refuse to approve a press release and then claim Talent didn't make a sufficient 'good faith effort'. The lack of specific standards for modification creates an opportunity for ESSI to act in bad faith, contradicting the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Michigan contract law. Although the urls are to commercial sources, the references and case law in those sources are sufficient to show the existence of good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: common law | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you common law Common law is law that is derived from judicial decisions instead of from statutes . American courts originally fashioned common law rules based on English common law until the American legal system was sufficiently mature to create common law rules either from direct precedent or by analogy to comparable areas of decided law. In the 2019 Supreme Court case of Gamble v. United States , Justice Thomas issued a concurring opinion discussing common law and, in particular, the role of stare decisis in a common law system. Though most common law is found at the state level, there is a limited body of federal common law--that is, rules created and applied by federal courts absent any controlling federal statute. In the 2020 Supreme Court opinion Rodriguez v. FDIC , a unanimous Court quoted an earlier decision to explain that federal "common lawmaking must be 'necessary to protect uniquely federal interests'" in striking down a federal common law rule addressing the distribution of corporate tax refunds. At the state level, legislatures often subsequently codify common law rules from the courts of their state, either to give the rule the permanence afforded by a statute, to modify it somehow (by either expanding or restricting the scope of the common law rule, for example) or to replace the outcome entirely with legislation. An example that gained national attention was the 2018 California Supreme Court decision in Dynamex Operations West, Inc. v. Superior Court , which articulated a three-part test for determining whether California workers were independent contractors or employees for purposes of California labor law. The California Legislature responded by creating a new section of the Labor Code, 2750.3 , which codified and expanded on the Dynamex holding and went into effect on January 1, 2020. Note that, like many statutes responding to a common law rule, California Labor Code Section 2750.3 specifically mentions the Dynamex holding. [Last reviewed in May of 2020 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex ACADEMIC TOPICS legal history CIVICS THE LEGAL PROCESS courts criminal law criminal procedure statutory interpretation wex definitions civil procedure government Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities regarding the Talent's obligation to obtain consent before publishing a press release, potentially contradicting the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The original text had a strict prohibition, but the modified text uses "good faith effort" which introduces wiggle room. The scraped snippet 1 defines common law as 'law that is derived from judicial decisions instead of from statutes', which means the implied covenant of good faith falls under this definition. The modification weakens the original clear obligation, creating a situation where ESSI could act unfairly, thus contradicting the implied covenant of good faith.
